🚩 **Starmer set to announce under-16s social media ban within 10 days — hardened position after parental consultation response.** The PM is preparing a ban on "harmful" platforms for under-16s (some "safer" social media access retained), with an announcement imminent.
*Why it matters:* A statutory under-16 ban reshapes Guided Childhood's entire addressable market — it strengthens the case for a "safer" supervised platform for 4–16s and could position us as a compliant alternative the moment the ban lands.

*Why it matters:* Sets a hard regulatory clock and an explicit expectation that platforms serving children build proactive image-detection controls — Guided Childhood should evidence these protections as a baseline feature now, ahead of competitors scrambling at the deadline.

**Children's Commissioner Dame Rachel de Souza publicly pushes to extend any ban to 16- and 17-year-olds — "cannot allow 16 and 17-year-olds to have lesser protection."** Her formal consultation response to "Growing up in the online world" landed this week.

**Internet Matters report assesses whether children are actually safer under the Online Safety Act — highlights remaining gaps from children's and parents' perspectives.** Ofcom's Children's Codes are now in active enforcement (July 2025 onward).
*Why it matters:* Independent evidence of where the OSA is "falling short" is precisely the gap Guided Childhood fills — cite this report directly in product and investor narratives to show we address documented unmet needs.

**Ofsted's new framework (report cards, five-point scale, inclusion focus) confirmed; from September 2026 RISE teams give 18 months' support to "stuck" schools.** Inspections now assess inclusion and wellbeing as core elements.
*Why it matters:* Schools under the new inclusion-and-wellbeing lens are prime channel partners — Guided Childhood's digital-literacy curriculum can be positioned as evidence for the inclusion/safeguarding elements inspectors now grade.
Source: https://www.tes.com/magazine/news/general/ofsted-first-report-card-inspections-voluntary

Guardian.output:
I have enough to identify the most significant development. Two strong candidates: the draft KCSIE 2026 (statutory, Sept 2026 implementation) and the NSPCC harmful online content briefing. For a DSL/Headteacher outreach hook, KCSIE 2026's strengthened filtering/monitoring + annual review requirement is the most actionable and time-sensitive.

---

**KEY SAFEGUARDING DEVELOPMENT**

**Draft Keeping Children Safe in Education (KCSIE) 2026 strengthens online-safety expectations — schools must now review the effectiveness of filtering and monitoring systems at least once every academic year, and embed online safeguarding as part of a whole-school culture rather than a standalone IT task.** Implementation is September 2026, with consultation ongoing now.
Source: https://schoolsweek.co.uk/kcsie-2026-everything-schools-need-to-know/ (also Geldards summary: https://www.geldards.com/insights/the-draft-keeping-children-safe-in-education-kcsie-2026-what-schools-academies-and-colleges-need-to-know/)

This lands alongside the NSPCC's February/March 2026 briefing *The Impact of Harmful Online Content on Children* (3,300+ Childline sessions; a 36% rise in counselling about online sexual abuse) — giving DSLs both a statutory deadline and a hard evidence base for acting now.
Source: https://learning.nspcc.org.uk/research-resources/2026/impact-harmful-online-content-children

**HOW GUIDED CHILDHOOD SHOULD RESPOND**

Position Guided Childhood as the tool that turns KCSIE 2026's "review effectiveness at least annually" requirement into an evidenced, repeatable process — giving DSLs the assurance records and whole-school visibility inspectors will expect from September. Frame it not as another filter, but as the audit and culture layer that proves online safeguarding is working, directly supported by the NSPCC's evidence of rising online harm. This lets schools walk into the new Ofsted framework's standalone safeguarding judgement with documentation ready, not scrambled.

YOUR ACTION TODAY: Draft a one-page "KCSIE 2026 filtering & monitoring review readiness" checklist mapping each new draft requirement to a Guided Childhood feature, so Navigator can attach it as the outreach hook for DSLs.
YOUR QUESTION: Has the final KCSIE 2026 wording on filtering/monitoring review frequency been confirmed post-consultation yet, or is the "at least once per academic year" line still draft (which changes how firmly Navigator can quote it)?

Sage.output:
News hook used: Starmer set to announce an under-16s social media ban within 10 days, with a distinction drawn between "harmful" platforms and retained "safer" social media access (Bloomberg, 9 June 2026).

## LINKEDIN VERSION

Chars: ~2,940

A ban is coming.

Within days, the Prime Minister is expected to announce restrictions on social media for under-16s — a hardened position after the parental consultation response.

And the first question in every staffroom will not be political.

It will be practical.

"What does this mean for Monday morning?"

Let me offer a calm reading, from someone who has sat on both sides of the safeguarding conversation.

First — read the language carefully.

Reporting suggests the ban will target "harmful" platforms while retaining access to "safer" social media for children.

That single distinction matters more than the headline.

Because it means the policy conversation is finally moving from "all or nothing" to "what does good look like for a child online."

That is the right conversation.

It is also the harder one.

A ban does not teach a fourteen-year-old how to handle a cruel comment.

A ban does not explain why a group chat turned nasty overnight.

A ban does not undo the fact that, for many children, the most important friendships of their week happen on a screen.

Regulation can reduce exposure.

It cannot, on its own, build judgement.

That work still lands where it always has — with teachers, with DSLs, with parents who are doing their best with imperfect information.

So here is the practical takeaway for school leaders this week.

One — do not wait for the speech to brief your community. A short, steady note to parents now ("we are watching this closely, here is our current advice") prevents panic later.

Two — separate the legal change from the educational need. Even if every harmful app vanished tomorrow, your digital literacy curriculum still matters. Children will be sixteen one day.

Three — resist the relief of thinking the problem is solved. The Online Safety Act was meant to make children safer, and Internet Matters' May report shows real gaps remain from children's and parents' own perspectives.

A ban is a floor, not a ceiling.

The schools that handle this well will not be the ones that simply comply.

They will be the ones that keep teaching children how to think, not just what to avoid.

That is slower work.

It is also the only work that travels with a child into adulthood.

If your school is reviewing how you talk to parents about all this, I am always happy to share what is working elsewhere.

This moment is an opportunity — not to lock children out of the digital world, but to walk them through it more deliberately.

Let's not waste it on fear.

What is the first question your parents will ask when the announcement lands?

#Safeguarding #DigitalLiteracy #EdLeadership #OnlineSafety #UKSchools

## SUBSTACK ARTICLE

# A ban is coming. Here is what it actually changes for your school.

Within the next ten days, according to Bloomberg, the Prime Minister is expected to announce restrictions on social media for under-16s — a hardened position arrived at after the response to the government's parental consultation. The reporting suggests a ban on "harmful" platforms, while some "safer" social media access is retained ([Bloomberg, 9 June 2026](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-09/uk-s-starmer-to-announce-under-16s-social-media-ban-within-days)).

If you are a DSL, a Headteacher or a PSHE lead, you have probably already had the corridor conversation. *Is this real? Does it apply to us? What do we tell parents?*

Let me try to answer those calmly, because the worst response to this news is a rushed one.

## What the policy actually says — and does not say

The crucial detail is buried in one word: "harmful."

Reporting indicates the ban will distinguish between "harmful" platforms and "safer" ones, retaining access to the latter. That is a significant shift from the binary framing — ban everything, or ban nothing — that has dominated this debate for two years.

It tells us the government is no longer asking *whether* children should be online. It is asking *what a child's online environment should be designed to do.*

That framing echoes the Children's Commissioner. Dame Rachel de Souza's consultation response this week argues platform features are too often "designed to increase harm," and she is pushing to extend protections to 16- and 17-year-olds, warning we "cannot allow 16 and 17-year-olds to have lesser protection" ([Children's Commissioner, June 2026](https://www.childrenscommissioner.gov.uk/resource/growing-up-in-the-online-world-a-national-consultation-response-from-the-childrens-commissioner/)).

So the direction of travel is clear: stronger statutory protection, a wider age band, and a focus on design rather than outright prohibition.

## Why a ban does not finish the job

Here is the uncomfortable part for any leader hoping this solves the problem.

It does not.

The Online Safety Act was supposed to make children safer, and Ofcom's Children's Codes have been in active enforcement since July 2025. Yet Internet Matters' May 2026 assessment of whether children are *actually* safer under the Act highlights persistent gaps from both children's and parents' perspectives ([Internet Matters, May 2026](https://www.internetmatters.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/Internet-Matters-Online-Safety-Act-Report-May-2026.pdf)).

A ban changes access. It does not change capability.

The child who is sixteen next year still needs to know how to recognise a scam, manage a falling-out in a group chat, and tell the difference between a real friend and a stranger performing one. Those skills are not switched on at a birthday. They are taught, modelled and revisited.

Regulation lowers the floor of harm. Education raises the ceiling of judgement. You need both.

## The separate clock you should not ignore

There is a second deadline running alongside the ban, and it matters for your safeguarding planning.

At London Tech Week on 8 June, the Prime Minister gave tech firms 90 days to deploy device-level controls that block children from sending or receiving nude images — naming Apple and Google specifically — or face legislation, fines and even executive criminal liability ([Reuters, 8 June 2026](https://www.reuters.com/business/media-telecom/big-tech-firms-must-stop-young-people-circulating-nude-images-says-uk-pm-starmer-2026-06-08/)).

For schools, this is a signal. Device-level image controls are coming, but they will arrive unevenly and late. Your safeguarding response to youth-produced imagery cannot wait for a vendor deadline. The DfE guidance and your existing escalation routes remain the backbone here.

## What to do in your school this week

You do not need a new policy by Friday. You need a steady hand. Four practical steps:

1. **Brief parents before the announcement, not after.** A short message — "we are aware of expected government changes, here is our current position, more to follow" — turns potential panic into reassurance. Communities forgive uncertainty; they do not forgive silence.

2. **Decouple the legal change from your curriculum.** Whatever the ban covers, your digital literacy teaching still matters. Map where in your PSHE and computing curriculum children actually practise judgement, not just rule-following.

3. **Audit your imagery-incident response.** Given the 90-day device-control deadline, revisit how staff respond to youth-produced sexual imagery now. Who is told, in what order, and how is the child supported rather than blamed?

4. **Prepare your governor line.** Expect questions at the next meeting. Be ready to say what you are doing on inclusion and online wellbeing — both of which Ofsted's new framework now grades as core elements from September 2026 ([Tes, 2026](https://www.tes.com/magazine/news/general/ofsted-first-report-card-inspections-voluntary)).

## The question worth sitting with

A ban will feel like an ending. It is closer to a beginning.

The schools that handle this best will not be the ones that simply comply. They will be the ones that keep teaching children to navigate, not just avoid.

So here is the question I would put to your leadership team this week:

*If every harmful app disappeared tomorrow, would your school still be teaching children how to think online — or only what to stay away from?*

---

*This briefing lands every weekday — the day's UK safeguarding and digital-childhood news, read through a school leader's lens, with something you can actually use. Subscribe to get it in your inbox each morning.*

## INSTAGRAM VERSION

A ban on social media for under-16s is coming within days. Here's what worries me.

I keep thinking about the fourteen-year-old in the back row, not the headline.

Because a ban changes what children can access. It doesn't change what they can handle.

The most useful thing in my old classroom was never a blocked app. It was the conversation afterward — the one where a child worked out, with help, why something online had made them feel small.

Regulation is a floor. Judgement is the ceiling. We still have to teach the ceiling.

So if the apps you fear most vanished tomorrow — would the children in your life still know how to think for themselves online?

I'd genuinely love your view below.

#DigitalParenting #OnlineSafety #Safeguarding #ParentingTeens #DigitalLiteracy #RaisingKids

## FACEBOOK VERSION

I was talking to a parent at the school gates last week, before any of this hit the news, and she said something that stuck with me: "I just want someone to make the decision for me."

I get it. Parenting online is exhausting.

So the news that the government is about to announce a social media ban for under-16s will, for a lot of families, feel like relief. Finally, a decision.

But here's the gentle thing I keep coming back to.

A ban can keep certain apps away from your child. It can't teach them how to handle a nasty group chat, or spot when someone online isn't who they say they are.

That part still falls to us. The conversations in the car. The questions at dinner. The "how did that make you feel?"

A ban is a helpful floor. It was never going to be the whole house.

What's your first reaction — relief, or something more complicated?

#OnlineSafety #Parenting

YOUR ACTION TODAY: Post LinkedIn before 8.30am (ahead of the expected PM announcement), paste Substack draft mid-morning, Instagram at 12pm, Facebook at 6pm — and hold a follow-up post ready to publish the moment the speech lands.
YOUR QUESTION: Will the announced ban define "safer" social media in a way that explicitly recognises supervised children's platforms — and do we want our public voice to lobby for that carve-out, or stay editorially neutral to protect Sage's trusted-educator credibility?

Format chosen: TRUST Framework — because Scout's lead finding is a ban/restriction story (Starmer's imminent under-16 ban + the Australian precedent), which is exactly the policy-mechanism territory the TRUST series exists to interrogate.

Series hook used: Scout's lead — Starmer set to announce an under-16 social media ban within 10 days (Bloomberg, 9 June 2026) — grounded against the Australian ban precedent, where six months on most observers agree teens are still getting in (nine.com.au, 29 May 2026; Euronews, 29 Jan 2026).

## PART 1 — LINKEDIN POST

Chars: ~2,980

60%.

That is roughly how many Australian teenagers were reportedly still reaching banned platforms within months of the world's first under-16 social media ban taking effect in December 2025.

Within days, our own Prime Minister is expected to announce a UK version.

Here is what the 60% figure IS.

It is hard evidence that prohibition changes the front door, not the building.

Here is what it ISN'T.

It is not proof the ban "failed" — and it is not permission to do nothing.

The signal is narrower and sharper than either side wants.

A ban moves children from supervised spaces into unsupervised ones — VPNs, borrowed logins, the open web — where no one is watching at all.

That is the displacement problem. Behaviour adapts. Harm relocates.

And here is the part the headline misses.

A ban is built for the average child.

But the average child was never the one most at risk.

When researchers report a small average effect of social media on wellbeing — Przybylski's Oxford work famously put it near the effect of wearing glasses — that average is small precisely BECAUSE it flattens two groups.

The majority, barely moved.

A vulnerable minority, hit hard.

A ban walls off the ninety-nine to reach the one — and in doing so, loses sight of exactly the one it was meant to protect.

Wrong villain does not mean no villain.

The platform rarely originates the harm. It amplifies a vulnerability the child already carried in.

So the real question is not "how do we lock the door."

It is "how do we keep sight of the child once they are through it."

That is what the TRUST framework is for.

T — Timing. Night-time use, not screen time, predicts most sleep and mood harm (Orben & Przybylski, Nature Human Behaviour).

R — Relationships. A trusted adult who asks "what happened online today" outperforms any filter.

U — Unsupervised access. Bans don't remove access; they remove visibility. The most dangerous space is the one no adult can see.

S — Surfacing the vulnerable. The minority hit hard is identifiable early — if anyone is looking.

T — Teaching judgement. A blocked app teaches avoidance. A guided conversation teaches navigation that travels into adulthood.

The Trust Framework series, continued.

Drop TRUST in the comments and I'll send you the full framework with every citation.

If this reframes the ban debate for you, please repost it — your network has a parent in it who needs this before the speech lands.

Follow me for the rest of the series.

One question I genuinely want a ban supporter to answer:

If the ban moves children somewhere no adult can see them, have we protected the vulnerable child — or just lost sight of her?

## PART 2 — SUBSTACK NEWSLETTER

# The 60% Problem: What Australia's Ban Tells Us About Britain's

*The deep-dive companion to today's LinkedIn post. For the researchers, DSLs and policy people who followed the comments and want the full evidence base — citations and all.*

This morning's post led with a single number: roughly 60% of Australian teenagers were reportedly still reaching banned platforms within months of that country's world-first under-16 social media ban, which took effect in December 2025.

The sharpest comment it drew — and it arrived fast — was the one I always expect on these posts:

> "You're using the small average effect to wave away real harm. Tell that to the parents of the kids who *were* damaged. The average is cold comfort."

That commenter is right, and I want to start there, because the answer to that objection is the entire reason the TRUST framework exists.

## Where this sits in the series

The Wrong Villain series has spent months on one argument: when we ask what drives harm to children's mental health, social media is rarely the largest term in the equation. Child poverty (roughly a third of UK children), adverse childhood experiences, and parental mental health all carry more variance than the small population-level signal attached to social media use. The peer-reviewed meta-analytic picture — 46 studies, 79 effect sizes in one recent synthesis — keeps landing in the same place: a small average association, often comparable in magnitude to mundane variables.

> The average is small precisely *because* it flattens two groups: the majority barely moved, and a vulnerable minority hit hard.

The TRUST framework is the constructive sequel. If the Wrong Villain series asks *what is actually driving the harm*, TRUST asks *what actually protects the child*. Today's ban news is the perfect test case, because a blanket ban is the policy that most completely ignores the distinction the evidence demands.

## What the 60% figure actually says

Let me be precise about the number, because precision is the whole point.

Six months after Australia's ban took effect, outlets that have tracked it closely — including nine.com.au's six-month review ([nine.com.au, 29 May 2026](https://www.nine.com.au/australia-news/social-media-ban-australia-how-the-under-16-legislation-is-working-six-months-later-20260529-p601wl.html)) and earlier reporting from Euronews documenting how quickly tech-savvy teens found workarounds ([Euronews, 29 Jan 2026](https://www.euronews.com/next/2026/01/29/australias-under-16-social-media-ban-how-children-are-already-finding-ways-around-it)) — converge on a blunt finding: large numbers of under-16s never actually left. Accounts were deactivated ([BBC explainer](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cwyp9d3ddqyo)); access was not.

What the figure **is**: evidence that prohibition changes the front door, not the building. It demonstrates *displacement* — children migrating from age-gated, moderated, parentally-visible platforms to VPNs, borrowed adult logins, and the unmoderated open web.

What the figure **is not**: proof the policy "failed" in some absolute sense, nor a licence for inaction. Reducing casual exposure for the majority may still be worthwhile. The point is narrower: a ban does not reach the children it was sold to protect, and it may move them somewhere worse.

The UK is now days from its own version, per [Bloomberg (9 June 2026)](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-09/uk-s-starmer-to-announce-under-16s-social-media-ban-within-days), reportedly distinguishing "harmful" from "safer" platforms — and the Children's Commissioner is pushing to extend protections to 16- and 17-year-olds ([Children's Commissioner, June 2026](https://www.childrenscommissioner.gov.uk/resource/growing-up-in-the-online-world-a-national-consultation-response-from-the-childrens-commissioner/)).

## The four sharpest objections — steelmanned, then answered

**1. "The small average effect hides a vulnerable minority. You're minimising real harm."**

Steelman: Aggregate effect sizes — like Przybylski and Orben's finding that the wellbeing association is in the region of the effect of wearing glasses or eating potatoes ([Orben & Przybylski, *Nature Human Behaviour*, 2019](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41562-018-0506-1)) — can lull policymakers into complacency while a tail of seriously harmed children goes unaddressed.

Rebuttal: This is not a counter-argument to me; it is my argument. A small mean across a population is *mathematically consistent* with severe harm concentrated in a minority — that is what flattening does. Wrong villain does not mean no villain. The error a ban makes is not denying the minority; it is designing for the average and losing sight of the minority entirely. You don't protect the vulnerable child by removing the platform; you protect her by maintaining the visibility to *see* her.

**2. "Effect-size interpretation depends on the statistical model."**

Steelman: Specification choices, self-report measures, and the direction of causality all move these numbers around (Ferguson's Smallest Effect Size of Interest critique, [ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0890856719314376)).

Rebuttal: Granted, honestly. Which is why I never lean on one model. Independent teams using different methods — UC Irvine's intensive longitudinal work, Cambridge MRC cognition research, Oxford's specification-curve analyses, Stetson's re-analyses — keep arriving at *small average, vulnerable tail*. When the method varies and the conclusion holds, the conclusion is doing the work, not the model.

**3. "A blanket ban cuts off isolated and LGBT kids who rely on these spaces as a lifeline."**

Steelman: For a queer teenager in an unsupportive town, an online community can be the only place they are understood. A ban can sever that.

Rebuttal: Correct, and this is the cruellest cost of the average-child approach. The ban walls off the ninety-nine to reach the one — and one of the ninety-nine it walls off is the isolated child for whom the platform is protective. What you actually need is *sight of who the one is* — the surfacing capability — not a wall that treats lifeline and threat as identical.

**4. "Platform data is behind a corporate gate, so independent research can't be trusted."**

Steelman: Most rigorous evidence is modelled from outside the platforms' walls, because the platforms won't open their logs.

Rebuttal: True, and it is a real limitation I won't paper over. But it cuts *against* bans as much as for them: if we can't see inside the platforms, building policy on the assumption that removing them solves the problem is equally unverified. The honest response to opacity is more independent visibility at the child level — not less.

## What this means for policy

A ban is a floor, not a ceiling. If the UK ban lands as reported — "harmful" out, "safer" retained — the single most important design question is whether "safer" is defined by *supervision and visibility*, or merely by content filtering. A filtered-but-invisible platform fails the surfacing test. The policy that follows the evidence would mandate not just what children can't see, but that a trusted adult can see *them*.

> The most dangerous online space is not the one with the worst content. It is the one no adult can see into.

It is also worth noting the parallel regulatory clock: Starmer's 90-day deadline for device-level image controls ([Reuters, 8 June 2026](https://www.reuters.com/business/media-telecom/big-tech-firms-must-stop-young-people-circulating-nude-images-says-uk-pm-starmer-2026-06-08/)), and Internet Matters' May 2026 finding that real gaps remain under the Online Safety Act ([Internet Matters](https://www.internetmatters.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/Internet-Matters-Online-Safety-Act-Report-May-2026.pdf)). The infrastructure is moving toward design and visibility — the ban is the bluntest instrument in a sharper toolkit.

## What parents and schools can do right now

- **Timing first.** Move the harm lever you actually control: get devices out of bedrooms overnight. Night-time use, not total screen time, carries the clearest sleep-and-mood signal.
- **Ask the relationship question daily.** "What happened online today?" beats any filter — and it keeps you in the loop the ban will push children out of.
- **Don't mistake the ban for the job.** Map where your curriculum *practises judgement*, not just rule-following. From September 2026, Ofsted grades inclusion and wellbeing as core ([Tes, 2026](https://www.tes.com/magazine/news/general/ofsted-first-report-card-inspections-voluntary)).
-

Herald.output:
I have enough to identify the strongest opportunity. The freshest, most under-served angle is the Guardian's 8 June story on the **"safer apps" carve-out** — campaigners urging Starmer to ban only apps that fail safety standards, not impose a blanket ban. This is the live, contested policy question Scout flagged, and it's distinct from Sage's "education over bans" educator angle.

---

**Scout finding used:** Starmer's imminent under-16 ban will distinguish "harmful" platforms from retained "safer" social media — the live question of how "safer" gets defined (Bloomberg, 9 June; reinforced by the Guardian's 20 May / 8 June reporting on campaigners urging a standards-based ban rather than a blanket one).

**Top PR opportunity:** The Guardian technology desk's running story — *"Limit social media ban for under-16s to unsafe apps, Starmer urged"* (Dan Milmo / Robert Booth, theguardian.com/technology, 20 May & 8 June 2026). This is a live, contested thread the moment the speech lands: campaigners want the ban scoped to apps that *fail* safety standards, which makes the definition of "safer" the central battleground. Sage is taking the educator-facing "a ban is a floor, not a ceiling" line, so the complementary, non-overlapping angle for media is the *policy-design* question: **what should "safer" actually mean, and who verifies it?** — a builder's-eye view a journalist can quote against ban-sceptic campaigners.

**Three-sentence pitch (send today):**

> Your reporting has nailed the question everyone's dodging: if the under-16 ban spares "safer" social media, who defines "safer" — and what stops it becoming a loophole? Our founder runs Guided Childhood, a supervised platform built for 4–16s, and can give you a concrete, on-the-record spec for what a genuinely "safer" children's platform must prove (age assurance, parental oversight, proactive image-detection ahead of Starmer's 90-day device-control deadline, and no engagement-maximising design the Children's Commissioner calls "designed to increase harm"). We can be on the phone within the hour of the PM's speech with a tight, quotable "five tests for a safe-by-design platform" checklist your readers can hold ministers and tech firms to.

YOUR ACTION TODAY: Email this pitch to Dan Milmo and Robert Booth at the Guardian technology desk this morning, headed "Reaction ready: who decides what 'safer' social media means?", with the five-test checklist attached and the founder on standby for the moment the speech lands.
YOUR QUESTION: Has the founder approved going on record advocating a standards-based "safer platform" definition — and are we comfortable being publicly seen to benefit commercially from the carve-out we'd be lobbying for?
